description: This Figure presents a comprehensive comparison of corrosion resistance and protective oxide film characteristics for Fe-30.34Mn-8.93Al-0.9C low-density steels processed under different heat treatment conditions. The dataset includes four distinct microstructural states: S0 (as-rolled without solution treatment), S950 (solution-treated at 950°C), S1050 (1050°C), and S1150 (1150°C), exhibiting average grain sizes from heterogeneous deformed structures to uniform equiaxed grains of 8.7 μm and 32 μm respectively. The plots display electrochemical parameters including breakdown current density (ranging from 2.72 μA/cm² for S950 to 9.92 μA/cm² for S0) and polarization resistance values, alongside oxide film properties such as thickness (16.2–25.2 nm), aluminum content (69.91–90.17 at%), and defect densities (ND 0.26–0.70×10²¹/cm³). The results demonstrate that fine-grained S950 achieves the lowest corrosion rate (0.160 mm/y) and forms the most protective Al-rich oxide film (90.17 at% Al, 25.2 nm thick), while coarse-grained S1150 exhibits inferior protection (0.281 mm/y, 69.91 at% Al). These findings substantiate that grain size optimization via solution treatment enhances corrosion resistance by promoting rapid, uniform formation of dense Al-rich oxide films at high-angle grain boundaries.

Figure 1 presents X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of Fe-30.34Mn-8.93Al-0.9C low-density steels (LDSs) subjected to different solution treatment conditions. Four curves are displayed: S0 (as-hot-rolled without solution treatment), S950 (solution-treated at 950°C), S1050 (solution-treated at 1050°C), and S1150 (solution-treated at 1150°C), all water-quenched after processing. The diffraction patterns confirm that all samples maintain a single-phase face-centered cubic (FCC) austenitic structure, indexed by the characteristic (111), (200), (220), and (311) reflections. Notably, the relative peak intensities vary systematically with solution treatment temperature: the (111) peak intensity increases significantly while the (220) peak intensity decreases correspondingly as the solution treatment temperature rises. These results indicate that solution treatment preserves the austenitic matrix while modifying the crystallographic texture. The absence of additional peaks confirms the dissolution of secondary phases during high-temperature solution treatment, establishing the foundation for subsequent grain size optimization and corrosion resistance studies.

This figure presents scanning electron microscopy (SEM) micrographs showing the microstructural evolution of Fe-30.34Mn-8.93Al-0.9C low-density steels (LDSs) after solution treatment at varying temperatures. Panel (a) depicts sample S0 in the as-hot-rolled condition, exhibiting a heterogeneous grain structure with deformed grains and high dislocation density. Panels (b)-(d) demonstrate progressive grain growth with increasing solution treatment temperature: S950 (950°C) shows fully recrystallized equiaxed austenite grains averaging 8.7 μm with abundant annealing twins; S1050 (1050°C) displays intermediate grain sizes between 8.7 and 32 μm; and S1150 (1150°C) exhibits coarse grains averaging 32 μm with significantly reduced grain boundary density. The images reveal that higher solution temperatures promote grain coarsening and reduce the density of high-angle grain boundaries and annealing twins, which directly influences the subsequent formation of protective Al-rich oxide films. This microstructural gradient establishes the foundation for understanding grain size-dependent corrosion resistance mechanisms.

This figure presents electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) characterization of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels (LDSs) subjected to solution treatments at varying temperatures. The panel comprises four rows representing samples S0 (as-rolled), S950 (950°C), S1050 (1050°C), and S1150 (1150°C), each containing three maps: inverse pole figure (IPF) maps (a-d) revealing grain orientation and morphology, kernel average misorientation (KAM) maps (a₁-d₁) indicating local residual stress distribution, and grain boundary (GB) distribution maps (a₂-d₂) distinguishing low-angle (2-10°, blue) and high-angle (>10°, red) boundaries. The IPF maps demonstrate that S0 exhibits heterogeneous grain sizes with deformed structures, while solution treatment produces equiaxed austenite grains that coarsen from approximately 8.7 μm (S950) to 32.0 μm (S1150). KAM analysis shows S0 has elevated residual stress (average 1.62) which reduces to ~0.4 after recrystallization. GB maps reveal S0 contains abundant LAGBs, whereas solution-treated samples display high densities of annealing twin boundaries (Σ3) and HAGBs in S950, progressively decreasing with grain growth. These microstructural modifications—particularly the optimized grain boundary density in S950—facilitate preferential aluminum dissolution at boundaries, enabling rapid formation of dense Al-rich oxide films that enhance corrosion resistance.

This figure presents quantitative microstructural analysis of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels (LDSs) subjected to different heat treatments. Subfigures (a)-(d) display grain size distribution histograms obtained via EBSD: (a) S0 (hot-rolled state) shows a bimodal distribution with an average grain size of 7.6 μm; (b) S950 (solution-treated at 950°C) exhibits a uniform distribution with 8.7 μm average grain size; (c) S1050 (1050°C) shows increased average grain size of 21.1 μm; and (d) S1150 (1150°C) displays coarse grains averaging 32.0 μm with extended tail up to 140 μm. Subfigure (e) compares grain boundary densities (left y-axis) and Kernel Average Misorientation (KAM) values (right y-axis, red line) across samples. S950 possesses the highest densities of annealing twin boundaries (ATBs, 0.146 μm/μm²) and high-angle grain boundaries (HAGBs, 0.114 μm/μm²), which progressively decrease in S1050 (0.061 and 0.029 μm/μm²) and S1150 (0.040 and 0.019 μm/μm²). The KAM value drops sharply from 1.62 in S0 to approximately 0.4 in all solution-treated samples, indicating significant reduction in residual stress via dynamic recrystallization. These data demonstrate that solution treatment homogenizes grain size while promoting ATB formation, with optimal grain boundary density achieved at 950°C, which correlates with enhanced Al-rich oxide film formation and superior corrosion resistance.

Figure 5 presents comprehensive TEM characterization of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels (LDSs), comparing the as-hot-rolled S0 sample with heat-treated variants. Panel (a) shows a bright-field TEM image revealing numerous nanoscale κ-carbides (5–10 nm in diameter, indicated by yellow arrows) dispersed in the austenitic matrix of S0, with the dashed green line highlighting a specific carbide region. The corresponding high-resolution TEM image and FFT pattern in (a1) confirm the crystalline structure of κ-carbide with zone axes [110]. Panel (a2) displays the high dislocation density in S0, with selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern confirming the single-phase austenite structure. Panel (b) illustrates the microstructural evolution after solution treatment, showing reduced dislocation density and the presence of annealing twin boundaries (ATBs) with corresponding SAED patterns. These images collectively demonstrate that the hot-rolled S0 sample contains coherent κ-carbides and high-density dislocations, while subsequent heat treatment promotes dislocation annihilation and twin formation, establishing the microstructural basis for grain size optimization and corrosion resistance studies.

This figure presents electrochemical corrosion characterization of low-density steels (Fe-30.34Mn-8.93Al-0.9C) with varying grain sizes. Panel (a) displays potentiodynamic polarization curves obtained in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution, plotting potential (V_SCE) versus current density (A/cm²) on a logarithmic scale for four samples: S0 (without solution treatment), S950 (solution treated at 950°C), S1050 (1050°C), and S1150 (1150°C). The curves reveal distinct passive behavior and breakdown characteristics across the different grain structures. Panel (b) quantifies the breakdown current density (i_bre) with error bars representing standard deviation, showing S0 exhibits the highest i_bre at 9.92 μA/cm², while the fine-grained S950 sample demonstrates the lowest i_bre of 2.72 μA/cm². Intermediate values are observed for S1050 (6.55 μA/cm²) and the coarse-grained S1150 (8.84 μA/cm²). The systematic increase in i_bre from S950 to S1150 correlates with grain coarsening, supporting the conclusion that fine grain structures promote rapid formation of protective Al-rich oxide films and superior corrosion resistance.

Figure 7 presents scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images showing surface corrosion morphology of Fe-30.34Mn-8.93Al-0.9C low-density steels (LDSs) after potentiodynamic polarization testing in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. Subfigure (a) displays the S0 sample (hot-rolled without solution treatment) exhibiting extensive corrosion damage with numerous deep pits, attributed to high surface residual stress and heterogeneous grain boundary distribution. Subfigure (b) shows the S950 sample (solution-treated at 950°C for 2 h) with only slight pitting corrosion and significantly smaller pit sizes, indicating superior corrosion resistance. Subfigure (c) illustrates the S1050 sample (solution-treated at 1050°C) with increased pitting features, including a magnified inset (10 μm scale) revealing detailed pit morphology. Subfigure (d) depicts the S1150 sample (solution-treated at 1150°C) demonstrating severe pitting corrosion expansion with enlarged pit walls. The comparison reveals that moderate grain size (~8.7 μm in S950) optimizes corrosion resistance through formation of dense Al-rich oxide films, while both ultrafine-grained S0 and coarse-grained S1050/S1150 exhibit degraded performance. Scale bars: 50 μm for (a), (b), (d); 100 μm for (c) main image.

This figure presents electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) data for Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels (LDSs) with different grain sizes tested in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution, comprising three subplots: (a) Nyquist plot showing the imaginary component (-Zim) versus real component (Zre) of impedance, where the capacitive arc diameter represents the polarization resistance; (b) Bode magnitude plot displaying the impedance modulus |Z| as a function of frequency; and (c) Bode phase plot showing the phase angle versus frequency. Four sample conditions are compared: S0 (as-hot-rolled without solution treatment), S950 (solution-treated at 950°C, 8.7 μm grain size), S1050 (solution-treated at 1050°C), and S1150 (solution-treated at 1150°C, 32 μm grain size). The EIS data reveal that S950 exhibits the largest capacitive arc in the Nyquist plot and highest impedance modulus, indicating superior corrosion resistance, while S0 shows the lowest resistance due to high dislocation density and κ-carbides. The Bode phase plot shows two time constants with maximum phase angles between 65° and 75° at intermediate frequencies, characteristic of non-ideal capacitive responses. An equivalent circuit model (inset in Fig. 8a) with two constant phase elements (Q1 and Q2) is employed for fitting, where Rs represents solution resistance, R1 and Q1 correspond to the diffusion resistance and CPE at the solution/film interface, and R2 and Q2 represent the oxide film resistance and CPE, respectively. The results demonstrate that fine grain structure (S950) promotes the formation of a dense, Al-rich (90.17 at%) oxide film with low defect density (ND 0.26×10²¹/cm³), providing optimal protection, whereas grain coarsening (S1050, S1150) reduces grain boundary density, leading to discontinuous oxide film growth with higher Fe/Mn content and degraded corrosion resistance.

This bar chart presents the corrosion rates of Fe-30.34Mn-8.93Al-0.9C low-density steels (LDSs) after 30 days of immersion in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution, comparing four samples with different thermomechanical processing histories. The x-axis labels denote: S0 (hot-rolled and water-quenched without solution treatment), S950 (solution-treated at 950°C for 2 h), S1050 (solution-treated at 1050°C for 2 h), and S1150 (solution-treated at 1150°C for 2 h). The y-axis quantifies corrosion rate in mm/y. The measured values reveal a non-monotonic trend with solution treatment temperature: S0 exhibits the highest corrosion rate at 0.326 mm/y, while S950 shows the minimum at 0.160 mm/y. The corrosion rate increases again with higher solution treatment temperatures, reaching 0.248 mm/y for S1050 and 0.281 mm/y for S1150. Error bars indicate experimental variability. This data supports the core conclusion that grain size optimization via solution treatment at 950°C (yielding ~8.7 μm equiaxed grains with high grain boundary density) significantly enhances corrosion resistance by facilitating the rapid formation of a dense, continuous Al-rich oxide film (90.17 at% Al, 25.2 nm thick). In contrast, the coarse-grained S1150 (32 μm) with lower grain boundary density exhibits reduced corrosion resistance due to discontinuous oxide film formation with higher defect densities and lower Al content (~69.91 at%).

This figure presents potentiostatic polarization curves showing current density versus time for Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels (LDSs) with different grain sizes in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. The main plot displays four curves corresponding to samples S0 (as-rolled, red), S950 (solution-treated at 950°C, green), S1050 (1050°C, blue), and S1150 (1150°C, magenta). All samples exhibit a rapid current density drop within the first 60 seconds to below 9 μA/cm², indicating initial oxide film formation, followed by fluctuations between 600-1400 s representing film rupture and self-healing. The inset magnifies the steady-state region (6400-7400 s), revealing that S950 maintains the lowest stable current density (~2.45 μA/cm²), while S0 shows the highest (~8.39 μA/cm²). The gradual increase in stable current density from S950 to S1150 reflects deteriorating corrosion protection with increasing grain size, demonstrating that optimized grain size via 950°C solution treatment enhances the formation of a protective Al-rich oxide film, significantly improving corrosion resistance compared to coarse-grained (S1150) and as-rolled (S0) conditions.

Figure 11 presents electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) characterization of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels (LDSs) after potentiostatic polarization in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution, comprising three panels: (a) Nyquist plot showing capacitive arcs, (b) Bode magnitude plot of impedance modulus versus frequency, and (c) Bode phase plot of phase angle versus frequency. The data compare four samples: S0 (as-rolled without solution treatment), S950 (solution-treated at 950°C), S1050 (1050°C), and S1150 (1150°C). Panel (a) reveals S950 exhibits the largest capacitive arc radius while S0 shows the smallest, indicating superior oxide film density and stability for S950. In panel (b), the low-frequency impedance modulus (0.01 Hz) decreases with increasing grain size, with S950 maintaining the highest value. Panel (c) shows S950 maintains a higher phase angle over a wider frequency range, confirming a more uniform oxide film structure. The equivalent circuit diagram (Rs(Q1(R1(Q2R2)))) in (a) was used for fitting. These results demonstrate that grain size optimization via 950°C solution treatment yields the highest polarization resistance (21355 Ω·cm²) and optimal corrosion resistance through formation of a dense Al-rich oxide film.

This figure presents Mott-Schottky analysis results for Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels (LDSs) with different grain sizes obtained via solution treatment at various temperatures. Panel (a) displays Mott-Schottky curves plotting C⁻² versus potential (E vs. SCE) for four samples: S0 (as-hot rolled, no solution treatment), S950 (950°C), S1050 (1050°C), and S1150 (1150°C). The curves reveal duplex semiconductor characteristics: Region 1 (-1.13 to -0.93 V_SCE) exhibits positive slopes indicating n-type behavior dominated by oxygen vacancies and cation interstitials, while Region 2 (-0.93 to -0.67 V_SCE) shows negative slopes characteristic of p-type behavior governed by cation vacancies. The S950 sample exhibits the highest C⁻² values, while S0 shows the lowest. Panel (b) quantifies the donor (N_D) and acceptor (N_A) concentrations calculated from the Mott-Schottky slopes. S950 achieves the lowest defect densities (N_D = 0.26×10²¹/cm³, N_A = 0.68×10²¹/cm³), significantly lower than S0 (N_D = 0.70×10²¹/cm³, N_A = 2.25×10²¹/cm³). S1050 (N_D = 0.38×10²¹/cm³, N_A = 1.03×10²¹/cm³) and S1150 (N_D = 0.49×10²¹/cm³, N_A = 1.25×10²¹/cm³) show intermediate values. These results demonstrate that grain size optimization via 950°C solution treatment minimizes structural defects in the Al-rich oxide film, thereby reducing chloride ion penetration pathways and enhancing corrosion resistance compared to both the as-rolled and higher-temperature treated samples.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra displaying the elemental composition and chemical state distribution of surface oxide films formed on Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels (LDSs) after 7200 s potentiostatic polarization in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. The figure comprises four rows of high-resolution spectra: Fe 2p₃/₂ (a-d), Mn 2p₃/₂ (a₁-d₁), Al 2p (a₂-d₂), and O 1s (a₃-d₃), comparing four samples with different grain sizes achieved via solution treatment at varying temperatures: S0 (as-rolled), S950 (950°C, fine-grained ~8.7 μm), S1050 (1050°C), and S1150 (1150°C, coarse-grained ~32 μm). The Fe 2p₃/₂ spectra reveal Fe²⁺ (709.8 eV), Fe³⁺_ox (711.5 eV), and Fe³⁺_hy (713.3 eV), with S950 uniquely exhibiting a Fe²⁺_sat satellite peak (715.0 eV) corresponding to FeO, Fe₂O₃, and FeOOH. Mn 2p₃/₂ spectra show Mn²⁺ (640.4 eV), Mn⁴⁺ (641.8 eV), and Mn²⁺_sat (646.2 eV) in S0, S1050, and S1150, attributed to Mn(OH)₂ and MnO₂, whereas S950 displays only the Mn²⁺ oxidation state. Al 2p spectra show a single Al³⁺ peak (74.1 eV) from Al₂O₃ and Al(OH)₃. O 1s spectra deconvolute into O²⁻ (529.7 eV), OH⁻ (531.6 eV), and H₂O (533.2 eV). The spectra demonstrate that S950 forms a dense, highly protective Al-rich oxide film (90.17 at% Al, 25.2 nm thickness) with low defect density (ND 0.26×10²¹/cm³), while S1150 exhibits reduced Al content (~69.91 at%) and higher Fe/Mn oxide incorporation. This supports the conclusion that fine-grained microstructures with high grain boundary density promote preferential Al dissolution and rapid formation of continuous protective oxide films, whereas coarse grains result in discontinuous, defective films with inferior corrosion resistance.

This figure presents a 3D stacked bar chart illustrating the relative cation fraction (at.%) of different valence states of metallic elements (Al³⁺, Fe²⁺, Fe³⁺ₒₓ, Fe³⁺ₕᵧ, Mn⁴⁺, Mn²⁺) in the surface oxide films of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels (LDSs). The quantitative data, derived from XPS peak area calculations using sensitivity factors, compares four samples: S0 (as-rolled), S950 (solution-treated at 950°C), S1050 (1050°C), and S1150 (1150°C). The visualization reveals that the fine-grained S950 sample exhibits the highest Al³⁺ content at 90.17 at.%, indicating a highly enriched aluminum oxide film. In contrast, the S0 sample shows significantly lower Al content (~50 at.%) with elevated contributions from iron and manganese species. As grain size increases with solution treatment temperature (from 8.7 μm in S950 to 32 μm in S1150), the Al³⁺ fraction progressively decreases to approximately 73% in S1150, accompanied by corresponding increases in Fe and Mn oxide contents. This trend demonstrates that grain refinement promotes preferential aluminum dissolution and rapid formation of a dense, protective Al-rich oxide film, whereas coarse-grained structures result in oxide films enriched with less-protective Fe and Mn species, thereby explaining the superior corrosion resistance of the S950 sample.

This figure presents Auger Electron Spectroscopy (AES) depth profiling results revealing the elemental distribution within surface oxide films of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels (LDSs) processed under different solution treatment conditions. Panel (a) shows four depth profiles for samples S0 (as-rolled), S950 (950°C), S1050 (1050°C), and S1150 (1150°C), plotting atomic concentrations of O, Fe, Al, and Mn versus sputtering time. The measured oxide film thicknesses, defined at half-maximum oxygen concentration, are 16.2±0.8 nm, 25.2±1.2 nm, 21.6±1 nm, and 18±0.9 nm respectively, indicating S950 possesses the thickest film. Panel (b) provides comparative elemental views, showing O and Fe profiles increasing or decreasing with depth across samples, while Al and Mn exhibit opposite trends. The S950 sample displays uniformly high Al content (~90 at.%) throughout the film thickness with minimal Fe/Mn incorporation, whereas coarser-grained samples (S1050, S1150) show declining Al and rising Fe contents with increasing sputtering depth. These results demonstrate that fine grain structures (8.7 μm) promote formation of dense, thick, Al-rich protective oxide films, while grain coarsening (32 μm) reduces film thickness and Al enrichment, compromising corrosion protection.

This figure presents the calculated E-pH (Pourbaix) diagrams for the Fe-Mn-Al-H₂O system at 25 °C, comprising three subplots showing the thermodynamic stability regions of (a) aluminum, (b) iron, and (c) manganese species. The diagrams were computed using HSC Chemistry 9 software with dissolved metal ion concentrations of 1×10⁻¹⁰ M for Fe, Mn, and Al at 1 atm pressure. Each subplot delineates stable phase fields including Al³⁺, Al₂O₃(H₂O), and AlO₂⁻ for aluminum; Fe³⁺, Fe₂O₃, Fe²⁺, FeO, and FeOH⁺ for iron; and Mn³⁺, MnO₄⁻, Mn²⁺, MnO₂, and MnO for manganese. Pink circles superimposed on all three diagrams indicate the experimental conditions of this study. The diagrams reveal that under the experimental conditions, aluminum resides within the stable zone of Al₂O₃, confirming its thermodynamic propensity to spontaneously form a stable, dense oxide film even under anaerobic conditions. In contrast, iron primarily stabilizes as Fe²⁺ with FeO as the main oxide, while manganese exists predominantly as Mn²⁺ with limited driving force for higher oxide formation. These thermodynamic calculations support the formation mechanism of protective Al-rich oxide films on low-density steels.

Figure 17 illustrates the initial corrosion dissolution behavior and surface potential distribution in Fe-30.34Mn-8.93Al-0.9C low-density steels with different grain sizes. SEM images (a) and (a1) for sample S950 (fine-grained, 8.7 μm) reveal extensive grain boundary dissolution along high-angle grain boundaries (HAGBs) and intragranular dissolution, while (b) and (b1) for S1150 (coarse-grained, 32 μm) show significantly reduced grain boundary attack due to lower GB density. SKPFM potential maps (c) and (d) display surface potential distributions across annealing twin boundaries (ATBs) and HAGBs, respectively. Graph (e) presents potential profiles along Line A (ATB) and Line B (HAGB), demonstrating that HAGBs exhibit lower potential (cathodic sites) while ATBs show higher potential compared to the matrix. These results indicate that fine-grained S950 with high GB density provides abundant active sites for preferential Al dissolution, facilitating rapid formation of dense Al-rich protective oxide films, whereas coarse-grained S1150 exhibits sparse dissolution and inferior corrosion resistance.

The figure presents the polarization resistance ($R_\mathrm{p}$) values derived from electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) fittings as a function of immersion time (0–168 h) for Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels (LDSs) subjected to different solution treatments. Four samples are compared: S0 (as-hot-rolled without solution treatment), S950 (solution-treated at 950°C), S1050 (1050°C), and S1150 (1150°C), represented by red stars, green circles, blue triangles, and pink squares respectively. The fine-grained S950 sample exhibits the highest $R_\mathrm{p}$ values, rapidly increasing to approximately $1.2\times10^5$ Ω·cm² within 24 h and stabilizing thereafter at 168 h (114.9 kΩ·cm²), indicating rapid formation of a dense, protective Al-rich oxide film. In contrast, the coarse-grained S1150 and S0 samples show significantly lower $R_\mathrm{p}$ values ($\sim$4–$5\times10^4$ Ω·cm²) with slower growth rates, suggesting delayed and less protective film formation. The S1050 sample displays intermediate performance ($\sim$8×10⁴ Ω·cm²). This demonstrates that grain refinement (8.7 μm for S950 vs. 32 μm for S1150) enhances corrosion resistance by promoting uniform oxide film formation through increased grain boundary density, which facilitates preferential Al dissolution and rapid development of a continuous protective barrier.

Figure 19 presents cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels (LDSs) solution-treated at different temperatures (S950: 950°C, S1050: 1050°C, S1150: 1150°C) in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. Panel (a) shows the first cycle where a distinct Al oxidation peak appears at approximately −0.81 V_SCE, with S950 exhibiting the highest peak current density as shown in the inset (~90 μA/cm²), while S1050 and S1150 display noticeably weaker peaks. Notably, no Fe oxidation peak is observed near −0.56 V_SCE in the first cycle. Panel (b) presents the second cycle, revealing an additional Fe/Mn oxidation peak near −0.56 V_SCE; here S950 shows the lowest peak intensity in this region, whereas S1050 and S1150 exhibit substantially enhanced anodic peaks. The curves demonstrate that fine-grained S950 with high grain boundary density facilitates preferential Al oxidation and rapid formation of a dense Al-rich oxide film during the initial stage, which subsequently suppresses Fe/Mn oxidation, while coarse-grained S1150 allows continued Fe/Mn participation in film formation due to incomplete surface coverage.

This figure presents schematic diagrams illustrating the oxide film formation and corrosion mechanisms in Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels (LDSs) with different grain sizes. Panel (a) depicts the small grain size sample, showing a high density of high-angle grain boundaries (HAGBs, blue lines) and annealing twin boundaries (ATBs, red lines). Subpanel (a1) demonstrates preferential anodic dissolution occurring at these grain boundaries, leading to localized formation of aluminum oxide (blue ellipses). Cross-sectional views (a2-a3) show that in a chloride-containing solution (orange spheres), this preferential dissolution results in rapid development of a continuous, dense Al-rich oxide film that provides effective protection. In contrast, panel (b) illustrates the large grain size sample with fewer grain boundaries. Subpanel (b1) reveals that the sparse grain boundary network leads to discontinuous oxide growth, with the formation of cracks and mixed oxides including iron oxide (red) and manganese oxide (purple). Cross-sectional views (b2-b3) confirm the formation of a defective oxide film with cracks and corrosion pits, offering inferior corrosion resistance compared to the fine-grained structure.
